turns.user_message: Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I've tried to quit three times before, and I just can't seem to stick to it. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just such a hard habit to break. I get really anxious and irritable when I try to quit, and the cravings are just so intense. I feel like I'm back at square one.
step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Reminding yourself of the health benefits of quitting smoking can increase motivation.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. You can now gently introduce ONE relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. Keep it simple — one idea at a time, check if they are open to it.

turns.user_message: Well, it's just...the anxiety is overwhelming. I feel like I'm on edge all the time, and I get so irritable with my friends and family. It's hard to resist.
step2_rag_chunks: Morning cravings are common for people who smoke regularly. Drinking water, brushing your teeth, or going for a short walk can help reduce the urge to smoke.
Exercise can help improve mood and reduce nicotine cravings.
Withdrawal symptoms from nicotine may include irritability, anxiety, difficulty concentrating, increased appetite, and strong cravings.

step2_rag_chunks: Replacing smoking with another activity such as chewing gum, eating healthy snacks, or walking can help break the habit.
Nicotine replacement therapy such as nicotine gum, patches, or lozenges can help reduce withdrawal symptoms and cravings.
Support from friends, family, or healthcare professionals can improve the chances of successfully quitting smoking.

Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?

Patient: Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I've tried to quit three times before, and I just can't seem to stick to it. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just such a hard habit to break. I get really anxious and irritable when I try to quit, and the cravings are just so intense. I feel like I'm back at square one.

Therapist: I can sense your frustration, Rachel. It sounds like you've been struggling with some tough withdrawal symptoms. Can you tell me more about what happens when you try to quit? What are some of the specific challenges you face?

Patient: Well, it's just...the anxiety is overwhelming. I feel like I'm on edge all the time, and I get so irritable with my friends and family. And the cravings...oh, the cravings are just awful. Therapist: I see. It sounds like those withdrawal symptoms are really getting in the way of your ability to quit. Have you thought about what might be different this time around? What might help you overcome those challenges?

Patient: I don't know...I feel like I've tried everything. I've used gum, patches, medication...nothing seems to work for me. And it's hard because my friends and colleagues all smoke, so it's hard to avoid temptation. I just feel like I'm never going to be able to quit for good.
